baseline_dominance.justification: The non-delegation flow achieves AS-side unlinkability post-enrollment — the AS/operator holds all operator private keys and credential fields yet cannot compute any scopedNullifier because scopeBlindingSecret is independently generated. This is structurally impossible in OAuth/OIDC (AS sees every token issuance), in BBS+ (issuer maintains correlation), and in WIMSE/SPIFFE (SPIRE operator sees SVID issuance). Multiple Tier 3 personas (rfc7662_advocate Attack 1/3, spiffe_engineer Attack 3) confirmed the baseline cannot match AS=operator adversarial unlinkability specifically. The rfc7662_advocate noted the §8 table overstates the hot-path claim but confirmed the AS-side property is genuine and unreachable by the baseline.

formal_security_argument.justification: Threat model (§3.1) is explicit: adversary controls AS/operator with all private keys, colluding RSes, on-chain observation. Named assumptions (A-PRF Poseidon, A-CR, A-KS PLONK, A-DL Baby Jubjub) are specific. Reduction sketch (§4.2 hybrid argument) is stated. However, cryptographer Attack 1 demonstrated an advantage-1 attack within the game's own stated rules: the adversary can legally query (j₀, S_b) in the query phase, then compare the deterministic scopedNullifier against the challenge proof π_b in world b=0. The game restriction is insufficient — it forbids (j₀, Sₐ) and (j₁, S_b) but permits (j₀, S_b), which is exactly the challenge pair in world b=0. Additionally, cryptographer Attack 2 identified that the A-PRF assumption claims Poseidon2 is symmetric in its arguments without justification — scopedNullifier keys s_b at position 2 while entryNullifier keys it at position 1, and no known result establishes joint PRF security across argument positions. These gaps make the reduction sketch not tight as stated.

implementability.justification: Every construction element maps to existing Bolyra primitives: Poseidon2/Poseidon5, EdDSAPoseidonVerifier on Baby Jubjub, BinaryMerkleRoot(20), PLONK with pot16.ptau, cumulative bit encoding. Circuit costs are stated: ScopedAgentAuth ~17,124 constraints, DelegationEntry ~16,928 constraints — both within 2^16 (65,536) pot16.ptau budget. Proving time targets (<3s PLONK, <2s Groth16 with rapidsnark) are within Phase 1 budget. §5 provides a complete primitive mapping table. No new primitives introduced — scopeBlindingSecret is a standard field element managed identically to the existing human secret scalar.

adversarial_survival.justification: Multiple Tier 3 attacks the construction cannot answer: (1) cryptographer Attack 1 — IND-UNL-AS game has advantage-1 attack within stated rules, game is broken as defined; (2) rfc7662_advocate Attack 2 — scopeCommitment = Poseidon2(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment) is an O(N) deanonymization oracle for delegation initiators since both inputs are adversary-known, directly contradicting the construction's claim that 'NFCU cannot determine which agent initiated the chain'; (3) cryptographer Attack 2 — dual PRF orientation is an unsubstantiated assumption gap; (4) cryptographer Attack 3 — scopeCommitment leaks the 8-bit permissionBitmask in O(256) work. The entryNullifier fix closed one O(N) oracle while leaving scopeCommitment as an identical oracle in the same circuit. These are not mitigated — they require redesign.

scenario_fit.justification: Two concrete scenarios are named with specific stakeholders: (1) NFCU member agent at Amazon/Costco where CU-as-AS must not learn merchant graph; (2) Kaiser Permanente patient agent across Quest Diagnostics and Cedars-Sinai. However, auth0_pm Attack 1 and cu_ciso Attack 1 identified that BSA/AML (31 U.S.C. §5318(g)) legally requires credit unions to monitor member transaction patterns and file SARs — the very property the construction provides (hiding merchant graph from CU) conflicts with the CU's regulatory obligations. The construction has no selective-disclosure or compliance-escrow mechanism. The healthcare scenario faces analogous HIPAA BAA requirements. The property is load-bearing in theory but creates compliance violations in the named deployments, making them hypothetical as described.

gaps: IND-UNL-AS game restriction must be extended to prohibit ALL four (challenge_agent, challenge_scope) pairs: (j₀, Sₐ), (j₀, S_b), (j₁, Sₐ), (j₁, S_b) — the current restriction permits (j₀, S_b) enabling an advantage-1 attack via deterministic nullifier comparison

scopeCommitment = Poseidon2(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment) must incorporate scopeBlindingSecret to close the O(N) deanonymization oracle for delegation initiators — both current inputs are adversary-known, making the entryNullifier fix incomplete

Dual PRF orientation must be resolved: either prove Poseidon2 is PRF-secure when the key appears in both argument positions, or normalize to a fixed key position via domain separation (e.g., Poseidon2(s_b, Poseidon2(0, scopeId)) and Poseidon2(s_b, Poseidon2(1, sessionNonce)))

Add a regulatory compliance mode — selective disclosure or compliance-escrow gadget that allows BSA/AML audit under legal trigger (subpoena, SAR threshold) without real-time surveillance, so the CU scenario is not structurally incompatible with 31 U.S.C. §5318(g)

Specify TEE/secure-enclave requirements for scopeBlindingSecret storage in cloud-native deployments where operator controls infrastructure — the trust boundary assumption does not hold in operator-managed Kubernetes/Lambda environments

scopeCommitment leaks the 8-bit permissionBitmask in O(256) Poseidon evaluations on-chain — must blind with scopeBlindingSecret or accept and document this as an explicit information leak in the delegation path
